questions.text:
[Section: Section I] Coloring a Banner
Difficulty: HARD | Max Score: 75
You have a banner that needs to be painted using a special brush with the following rules:
The brush can only paint one type of color in a single stroke.
In one stroke, you can start and stop at any position on the banner, and it will overwrite any existing colors in that range.

You are given a string S, where each character represents a different color on the banner, return the minimum number of strokes required to completely paint the banner.

Input Format:
A single string S, consisting of lowercase English letters, representing the banner colors.

Output Format:
A single integer representing the  required to completely paint the banner.

Constraints:
1 ≤ s.length ≤ 100
s consists of lowercase English letters only.


Example 1:
Input:
aaabbb

Output:
2

Explanation:
Stroke 1: Paint "aaa" first.
Stroke 2: Paint "bbb".

Thus, 2 strokes are required.


Example 2:
Input:
aba

Output:
2

Explanation:
Stroke 1: Paint "aaa" first.
Stroke 2: Paint "b" at the middle position, overwriting the "a" in that spot.

Thus, 2 strokes are required.

[Section: Section II] Customer Satisfaction Analysis
Difficulty: HARD | Max Score: 75
A retail chain tracks the daily customer satisfaction scores to evaluate its performance. To analyze trends, they use a sliding window approach.
For any chosen window size, the following steps are performed:
Compute the maximum (peak score) in every contiguous subarray (window) of that size.
Among all these peak scores, determine the weakest peak, i.e., the minimum of all maximum values.

Your task is to efficiently compute the weakest peak for multiple given window sizes.

Input Format:
The first line contains an integer N, the number of days.
The second line contains an integer Q, the number of queries.
The third line contains N space-separated integers, representing the satisfaction scores.
The next Q lines each contain a single integer K, representing the window size.

Output Format:
Print Q integers, each on a new line, where each integer represents the weakest peak for the corresponding window size.

Constraints
1 ≤ N ≤ 105
1 ≤ Q ≤ 100
1 ≤ satisfaction_scores[i] ≤ 106
1 ≤ K ≤ N


Example1:

Input:
n = 3  
scores = [50, 19, 27]  
queries = [3, 1]

Output:

[50, 19]

Explanation:
For window size 3 → max in [50,19,27] is 50
For window size 1 → peaks are [50,19,27] → weakest = 19


Example2:

Input:
3
5
50
19
27
3
3
1
1
1

Output:
50
50
19
19
19

Explanation:
for window size =3, peaks are: [50]. Min of these=50
for window size =1, peaks are: [50,19,27]. Min of these=19

[Section: Section III] ABC of Alice
Difficulty: EASY | Max Score: 25
Alice is setting up a new password of length n using only the first three lowercase English letters: a, b and c.
To make the password secure, the system enforces two strict rules:
No two adjacent characters can be the same. (e.g., aa or bb are forbidden).
No three consecutive characters can form a palindrome. (e.g., aba or bcb are forbidden).
Since Alice wants to remember her password easily, she decides to choose the lexicographically smallest password that satisfies both rules.
Given n, your task is to output Alice's password.
(Note: A string S is lexicographically smaller than string T, if at the first position where they differ, the character in S comes earlier in the alphabet than the character in T.)

Input:
The only line contains a single integer N (length of the password).

Output:
For each test case, output a single string of length N representing the lexicographically smallest valid password.
 Example 1:
Input: 1
Output: a
Explanation: Both the rules are being followed in this password and it is the lexicographically smallest password possible out of the correct passwords.

Example 2:
Input: 3
Output: abc
Explanation: Both the rules are being followed in this password and it is the lexicographically smallest password possible out of the correct passwords.
 Constraints:
1 <= N <= 106

Input:

 Example 1:

[Section: Section III] ABC of Alice
Difficulty: EASY | Max Score: 25
Alice is setting up a new password of length n using only the first three lowercase English letters: a, b and c.
To make the password secure, the system enforces two strict rules:
No two adjacent characters can be the same. (e.g., aa or bb are forbidden).
No three consecutive characters can form a palindrome. (e.g., aba or bcb are forbidden).
Since Alice wants to remember her password easily, she decides to choose the lexicographically smallest password that satisfies both rules.
Given n, your task is to output Alice's password.
(Note: A string S is lexicographically smaller than string T, if at the first position where they differ, the character in S comes earlier in the alphabet than the character in T.)

Input:
The only line contains a single integer N (length of the password).

Output:
For each test case, output a single string of length N representing the lexicographically smallest valid password.
 Example 1:
Input: 1
Output: a
Explanation: Both the rules are being followed in this password and it is the lexicographically smallest password possible out of the correct passwords.

Example 2:
Input: 3
Output: abc
Explanation: Both the rules are being followed in this password and it is the lexicographically smallest password possible out of the correct passwords.
 Constraints:
1 <= N <= 106
